steps.title: Factor the quadratic
steps.content: To factor $5x^2 + 31x - 28$, look for two binomials $(5x + a)(x + b)$ where $ab = -28$ and $5b + a = 31$. Testing $a = -4, b = 7$: $(5x - 4)(x + 7) = 5x^2 + 35x - 4x - 28 = 5x^2 + 31x - 28$. ✓

steps.title: Test expression I using the Factor Theorem
steps.content:
By the Factor Theorem, $(x - 7)$ is a factor if and only if $f(7) = 0$.
$$f(7) = 5(7)^2 + 31(7) - 28 = 245 + 217 - 28 = 434$$
Since $f(7) = 434 \neq 0$, expression I is NOT a factor.

steps.title: Test expression II using the Factor Theorem
steps.content:
By the Factor Theorem, $(5x - 4)$ is a factor if and only if $f(4/5) = 0$ (setting $5x - 4 = 0$ gives $x = 4/5$).
$$f(4/5) = 5\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^2 + 31\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) - 28 = \frac{16}{5} + \frac{124}{5} - 28 = \frac{140}{5} - 28 = 0$$
Since $f(4/5) = 0$, expression II IS a factor. Only statement II is a factor. That is the correct answer.

choiceAnalysis:
• Choice A — I only: Expression I is $(x - 7)$. Testing $f(7) = 5(49) + 217 - 28 = 434 \neq 0$, so $x - 7$ is not a factor. ✗
  • Choice B — II only: Expression II is $(5x - 4)$. Testing $f(4/5) = 16/5 + 124/5 - 28 = 0$ ✓. Expression I fails as shown. Only II is a factor. ✓
  • Choice C — I and II: Expression I is not a factor ($f(7) = 434$), so both cannot be factors. ✗
  • Choice D — Neither I nor II: Expression II is confirmed a factor via the Factor Theorem. ✗
That is the correct answer.
